senses.gloss: the yield of flowering plants, botanical fruits, crops

examples.text: وَهُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَ جَنَّاتٍ مَّعْرُوشَاتٍ وَغَيْرَ مَعْرُوشَاتٍ وَالنَّخْلَ وَالزَّرْعَ مُخْتَلِفًا أُكُلُهُ وَالزَّيْتُونَ وَالرُّمَّانَ مُتَشَابِهًا وَغَيْرَ مُتَشَابِهٍ ۚ كُلُوا مِن ثَمَرِهِ إِذَا أَثْمَرَ وَآتُوا حَقَّهُ يَوْمَ حَصَادِهِ ۖ وَلَا تُسْرِفُوا ۚ إِنَّهُ لَا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْرِفِينَ
examples.english: He is the One Who produces gardens—both cultivated and wild—and palm trees, crops of different flavours, olives, and pomegranates—similar in shape, but dissimilar in taste. Eat of the fruit they bear and pay the dues at harvest, but do not waste. Surely He does not like the wasteful.

senses.gloss: the yield of flowering plants, botanical fruits, crops; the typically sweet- or (less commonly) sour-tasting produce of plants, fruit, fruitage

the yield of flowering plants, botanical fruits, crops; something or someone resembling, likened to, or related to fruit (such as natural produce, the result of a happening or an action, offspring or progeny, the produce of anything, the fruit of one's labor, gain, advantage, profit, possessions, riches, money, and so on)
the knots of the whiplash that lacerate the skin; the thong

etymology.text: Inherited from Proto-Semitic *ṯamar- (“fruits, crops”). Internally in Arabic the source of the root ث م ر (ṯ m r). Doublet of تَمْر (tamr, “dates”).
